summary:
The dental pulp is primarily innervated by sensory afferents from the maxillary and mandibular divisions of the trigeminal nerve (cranial nerve V). Although autonomic fibers (sympathetic and parasympathetic) regulate vascular tone within the pulp, they do not provide sensory input and thus are irrelevant to questions concerning pulpal sensitivity or diagnosis. 

(Note: Final answer choice confirmed as \boxed{A}.)

The dental pulp is sensitively innervated only by the maxillary and mandibular branches of the trigeminal nerve (Cranial Nerve V), which convey sensations such as temperature, pressure, and pain. Other cranial nerves—such as the facial, glossopharyngeal, or hypoglossal—are not responsible for sensory innervation; while minor autonomic inputs affect vascularity, they play no role in detecting stimulus-related changes in the pulp.  

(Answer: \boxed{A})  
--- 
End of Reasoning Chain
